text: Q4_K_M barely moves perplexity on Llama-3-8B — I measured 6.14 vs 6.09 at fp16 on wikitext. The K-quants keep the salient weights in higher precision, which is why the gap is so small.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Switched llama.cpp -> vLLM on my 3090 and Qwen2.5-7B went from 34 to 61 tok/s at batch 1. Paged attention cuts the KV-cache fragmentation, that's most of the win.
label: ANALYSIS

text: The MMLU number people keep quoting for this model is the 5-shot CoT score. The 0-shot in the paper's appendix is 8 points lower, so head-to-head with GPT-4 isn't apples to apples.
label: ANALYSIS

text: On a single 24GB card you can't fit 70B at Q4 with any real context — KV cache for 8k ctx alone is ~2.5GB and weights are already ~20GB. You need offload or a second card.
label: ANALYSIS

text: exl2 at 4.65bpw fits 34B in 24GB with 16k context for me, and quality is basically indistinguishable from 5bpw on my eval set (78% vs 79% on my 50-question rubric).
label: ANALYSIS

text: Speculative decoding with a 1B draft model got me ~1.8x on Llama-3-70B because the draft accepts ~70% of tokens on my workload (mostly code). On creative writing acceptance drops and the speedup nearly vanishes.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Flash attention 2 isn't supported on P40s — they're Pascal, no bf16 and no FA2 kernels. That's why your 3090 crushes the P40 even at the same VRAM.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Rope scaling to 32k degrades needle-in-haystack recall past ~16k in my tests; retrieval accuracy fell from 95% to ~60% at the far end. The model wasn't trained that long so it's extrapolating.
label: ANALYSIS

text: I ran the same 200 prompts through Gemma-2-9B and Qwen2.5-7B. Qwen won on the coding subset (41/50 vs 33/50) but Gemma was better on the summarization ones (44/50 vs 38/50).
label: ANALYSIS

text: AWQ vs GPTQ on my setup: AWQ was ~10% faster decode on the 4090 and matched GPTQ on accuracy, because AWQ keeps activation-aware salient channels in fp16 so the kernel stays simpler.
label: ANALYSIS

text: The reason your tok/s tanks at long context is the KV cache, not the weights. It grows linearly with sequence length, so by 16k you're memory-bandwidth bound. Quantizing the KV cache to Q8 got me back ~20%.
label: ANALYSIS

text: DeepSeek-V2's MoE only activates 21B of 236B params per token, which is why it runs faster than a dense 70B despite the huge total size — fewer FLOPs per forward pass.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Tested temperature 0 vs 0.7 on a JSON extraction task: temp 0 gave 49/50 valid JSON, temp 0.7 dropped to 41/50. For structured output low temp is just better, sampling adds nothing.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Mistral-7B-v0.3 has a 32k context but it's sliding-window attention with a 4k window, so true long-range recall is weaker than the context number suggests. I saw recall drop sharply past 4k.
label: ANALYSIS

text: My RAG latency was dominated by the embedding step, not the LLM. Switching from a 7B embedder to bge-small (33M) cut retrieval from 800ms to 60ms with no measurable hit to recall@5.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Llama-3 uses a 128k vocab vs Llama-2's 32k, so it encodes the same text in fewer tokens — I see ~15% fewer tokens on English, which is effectively a free context and speed boost.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Batching is why vLLM beats llama.cpp on a server: at batch 32 the 4090 stays compute-bound and hits ~2000 tok/s aggregate, while llama.cpp single-stream leaves the GPU idle between tokens.
label: ANALYSIS

text: I fine-tuned with LoRA rank 16 vs rank 64 on the same 2k examples. Rank 64 overfit (val loss climbed after epoch 2) while rank 16 kept improving. For a small set, lower rank generalized better.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Q2_K is genuinely bad — perplexity on Llama-3-8B jumped from 6.1 (Q4) to 9.8 (Q2). Below Q3 you're trading away real capability, the model starts dropping instructions.
label: ANALYSIS

text: The 'it's slow on Ollama' complaints are usually because Ollama defaults to a 2048 context and reloads the model on each call. Set num_ctx and keep_alive and most of that disappears — went from 4s to 0.3s overhead for me.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Phi-3-mini punches above its size on benchmarks because it's trained on heavily filtered 'textbook' data, but I find it brittle on anything off-distribution — it failed 7/10 of my real support tickets while Llama-3-8B handled them.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Offloading 20 of 33 layers to GPU on a 12GB card got me 11 tok/s on a 13B; full CPU was 2.3. The bottleneck is the CPU<->GPU transfer per token for the offloaded layers, so partial offload has diminishing returns.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Grouped-query attention is why Llama-3-70B's KV cache is so much smaller than you'd expect — 8 KV heads instead of 64, so the cache is 8x smaller than full multi-head at the same context.
label: ANALYSIS

text: I benchmarked llama.cpp's Q4_0 vs Q4_K_M: K_M was ~5% slower decode but clearly better quality on my reasoning prompts (got 3 more of 20 right). The extra block-wise scales are worth the small speed cost.
label: ANALYSIS

text: CodeQwen-7B beat DeepSeek-Coder-6.7B on my internal HumanEval-style set (38/40 vs 34/40), but DeepSeek was better at filling in the middle. They were trained with different FIM objectives, which shows.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Setting --n-gpu-layers too high silently spills to shared memory on Windows and tanks speed instead of erroring. I dropped from 33 to 28 layers and tok/s tripled because it stopped paging.
label: ANALYSIS

text: The reason Q4 quantized models sometimes loop is that quantization flattens the logit distribution slightly, so with greedy decoding ties resolve to repeats. A small repetition penalty (1.1) fixed it for me.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Yi-34B's long-context claim held up better than most in my testing — it actually recalled facts at 32k (90%+) where a lot of 'long context' models collapse. They continued pretraining at length rather than just rope-scaling.
label: ANALYSIS

text: LoRA merges back into the base with zero inference overhead because it's just W + BA folded into W. That's the whole point — you only pay the cost during training, not at serve time.
label: ANALYSIS

text: On an M2 Max the unified memory lets you run 70B Q4 at ~8 tok/s, which a 16GB discrete GPU just can't touch for that model size. Bandwidth is ~400GB/s, that's what's carrying it.
label: ANALYSIS

text: I A/B'd two system prompts on 100 chats: the concise one had 12% fewer refusals and identical task success. Long 'you are a helpful assistant...' preambles mostly waste context and nudge it toward hedging.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Quantizing the KV cache to Q4 saved me ~40% VRAM at 32k context but dropped long-range recall noticeably (needle test fell ~15 points). Q8 KV was the sweet spot — half the savings, no measurable quality loss.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Gemma-2 uses logit soft-capping during training; if you run it without the cap in an old llama.cpp build the outputs degrade. Updating to a build that implements capping fixed the gibberish I was seeing.
label: ANALYSIS

text: The 70B feels 'smarter' than the 8B on ambiguous instructions specifically because of in-context reasoning depth — on my 30 trick prompts the 70B got 24 vs the 8B's 14. Size buys robustness, not just facts.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Continuous batching is the single biggest server-throughput lever. Same 4090, same model: static batching gave ~900 tok/s aggregate, continuous batching ~1700, because finished sequences free slots immediately.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Mixtral 8x7B is ~13B active params, so it decodes at roughly 13B speed (~45 tok/s on my 3090 with offload) while answering like something bigger. The tradeoff is you still need VRAM for all 47B of weights.
label: ANALYSIS

text: I tried int8 vs Q4 for a classification fine-tune: int8 was barely better on accuracy (91 vs 90) but 2x the VRAM. For deployment on one card Q4 was clearly the right call.
label: ANALYSIS

text: nous-hermes finetunes follow multi-turn instructions better than the base in my testing because they're trained on long assistant transcripts; base Llama drifts off the format by turn 4, the finetune holds to turn 10+.
label: ANALYSIS

text: The '128k context' on most local models is theoretical — past ~32k my throughput drops below 5 tok/s and recall degrades, so effective usable context on a 24GB card is way under the spec number.
label: ANALYSIS

text: DRY sampling killed the repetition problem better than rep-penalty for me because it penalizes repeated sequences not just tokens, so it stops the model looping whole phrases while leaving normal word reuse alone.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Running two 3090s with tensor parallelism on vLLM nearly doubled throughput on 70B (NVLink helps), but with llama.cpp's layer split I only got ~1.3x because the layers run sequentially across cards, not in parallel.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Whisper large-v3 is overkill for clean English — distil-large-v2 ran 4x faster on my 3060 with <1% WER difference on my test audio. The distilled model drops decoder layers that barely matter for clear speech.
label: ANALYSIS

text: The instruct tuning is why base models 'can't chat' — base Llama-3 completes text, it was never trained to answer. Feed it a few-shot Q/A format and it works fine, which proves it's a format issue not a capability one.
label: ANALYSIS

text: EXL2's measurement step lets you mix bitrates per layer, so 4.0bpw EXL2 often beats a flat 4-bit GPTQ on quality — it spends more bits on the layers that need them. I saw a clear win on my perplexity check.
label: ANALYSIS

text: I measured prompt-processing vs generation separately: on a 4090 prompt eval is ~3000 tok/s but generation is ~80 tok/s, because prefill is parallel over the prompt while decode is one token at a time.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Llama-3-8B-Instruct refuses way less if you don't include the default safety preamble — I ran 50 borderline-but-benign prompts and refusals dropped from 18 to 4 just by trimming the system prompt.
label: ANALYSIS

text: The reason your finetune 'forgot' general knowledge is catastrophic forgetting — full fine-tuning on a narrow set overwrites the base. LoRA avoided it for me because the base weights stay frozen.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Qwen2.5-Coder-32B actually matched GPT-4o on my repo-level tasks (passed 17/20 vs 18/20), specifically on the ones needing the whole-file context. Smaller coders failed those because they lost track past a few hundred lines.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Speculative decoding gives zero benefit if your draft and target tokenizers differ — the tokens don't align so nothing gets accepted. They have to be from the same family, which is why people pair 1B+70B Llama.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Setting n_batch higher in llama.cpp sped up my prompt processing a lot (512 -> 2048 took prefill from 9s to 3s on a 4k prompt) but didn't touch generation speed, since that's still token-by-token.
label: ANALYSIS

text: I compared Q5_K_M and Q6_K on a 13B and honestly couldn't measure a quality difference (both 16/20 on my reasoning set), so Q5 is the better pick — same quality, less VRAM, slightly faster.
label: ANALYSIS

text: The Tesla P40 is cheap-per-GB but its fp16 is gimped (1:64 ratio), so it's only good for fp32/int8 paths. That's why people report it being slow despite the 24GB — the math units aren't there for modern kernels.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Llama-3.1's 128k context actually worked in my long-doc QA (recalled details at 100k+, ~85%) where 3.0 fell apart past 8k. They did the long-context continued pretraining properly this time.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Reducing max_tokens doesn't speed up per-token generation, it just stops sooner — people confuse the two. Your tok/s is fixed by the model+hardware; capping length only caps total wall time.
label: ANALYSIS

text: FP8 on Ada cards (4090) is real hardware acceleration, not emulated, so FP8 inference there is genuinely fast. On a 3090 (Ampere) FP8 falls back to emulation and you lose the benefit — checked with nvidia-smi util.
label: ANALYSIS

text: My agent loop was slow because it re-sent the whole history each step; switching to prompt caching cut prefill cost ~80% since the static system+tools prefix gets reused instead of recomputed every turn.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Gemma-2-27B beat Llama-3-8B on my multilingual eval by a wide margin (German/Spanish accuracy ~20 points higher) — it's the larger multilingual pretraining mix, the 8B is much more English-centric.
label: ANALYSIS

text: I quantized my own finetune with imatrix calibration on in-domain text and the Q4 came out noticeably better than a generic Q4 (2 more right of 20) because the importance matrix matched my actual use case.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Running the model with --no-mmap actually helped on my machine with fast RAM because mmap was causing page faults under memory pressure; load was slower but generation was steadier. YMMV with slow disks.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Llama-3-70B at IQ2_XXS fits in 24GB and is shockingly usable — got 14/20 on my reasoning set vs the 8B Q8's 11/20. The i-quants with imatrix preserve more than the old Q2_K at the same size.
label: ANALYSIS

text: The 'tiny model beats GPT-4' headlines are almost always one cherry-picked benchmark. On a broad 6-task suite the 7B trailed by 15+ points on every task except the one it was tuned for. Read the full table, not the tweet.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Tokenizer matters for non-English: Llama-3 uses ~2x fewer tokens than Llama-2 on Japanese in my test, so the same context window holds twice the text and costs half as much to process.
label: ANALYSIS

text: vLLM's prefix caching gave me a big win on a chatbot with a fixed long system prompt — TTFT dropped from 1.2s to 0.2s on repeat calls because the shared prefix's KV is cached instead of recomputed.
label: ANALYSIS

text: I tested 'thinking' prompts on Qwen: adding 'reason step by step' lifted my logic-puzzle accuracy from 12/25 to 19/25, but had no effect on factual recall questions. CoT helps reasoning, not memory.
label: ANALYSIS

text: ExLlamaV2 is faster than llama.cpp on pure-GPU 4090 runs in my tests (78 vs 64 tok/s on a 13B) but llama.cpp wins the moment you need CPU offload, which ExLlama doesn't really do.
label: ANALYSIS

text: My OOM at 'only' 20GB used was fragmentation — PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF=expandable_segments let the same workload fit. The allocator was holding fragmented blocks it couldn't reuse for the big KV tensor.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Distilling my 70B's outputs into a 7B LoRA actually transferred most of the task quality (7B finetune hit 88% of the 70B's score on my eval) — the small model just needed the right supervision, not more params.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Since Q4 preserves the outlier channels in higher precision, why does my 70B still OOM at 24GB? Because the weights are ~20GB and the 16k KV cache adds ~5GB — quantizing weights doesn't shrink the cache.
label: ANALYSIS
notes: Q from M1 'questions' edge case: embeds a real supported claim -> ANALYSIS

text: BitsAndBytes 4-bit (nf4) was slower for me than a GGUF Q4 at the same quality — nf4 dequantizes on the fly in fp16 every forward pass, while GGUF kernels operate on the quantized blocks directly.
label: ANALYSIS

text: I ran the lmsys-style pairwise eval locally and Llama-3-70B won 58% vs Mixtral on my 80 prompts, mostly on instruction-following — Mixtral was competitive on knowledge but drifted from the asked format more often.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Llama-3.1-8B at fp16 is ~16GB of weights (8B params x 2 bytes), so on a 12GB card you literally can't load it unquantized — that's just the arithmetic, not an opinion.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Tested RAG chunk sizes: 256 tokens gave cleaner retrieval but split facts across chunks and hurt answer completeness; 384 was my sweet spot on a 60-question eval (best F1).
label: ANALYSIS

text: Bumping vLLM --max-num-seqs from 16 to 64 doubled aggregate throughput on my 4090 (≈900 -> 1800 tok/s) because the GPU stayed saturated instead of idling between requests.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Gemma-2-2B runs ~90 tok/s CPU-only on my i7 — I timed it — which makes it the only model here actually usable without a GPU.
label: ANALYSIS

text: GGUF embeds the tokenizer and metadata in the file, which is why a single .gguf 'just works' in llama.cpp while safetensors needs a separate config.json and tokenizer.json.
label: ANALYSIS

text: On 100 math word problems, 'think step by step' lifted Qwen2.5-7B from 52% to 71% but did nothing for Llama-3-8B (~55% both ways). CoT gains are model-dependent, I measured both.
label: ANALYSIS

text: The 7B felt dumber after my finetune because I trained 8 epochs on 500 examples — classic overfit, train loss hit ~0 while val loss climbed after epoch 3. Dropped to 2 epochs and it recovered.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Embedding 50k docs with bge-large took 40 min on my 3060; switching to bge-small cut it to 9 min with recall@10 dropping only 1 point on my test queries. Smaller embedder was the right trade.
label: ANALYSIS

text: llama.cpp's --flash-attn gave me ~15% faster prefill on a 4090 and lower VRAM at long context, because FA avoids materializing the full attention matrix.
label: ANALYSIS

text: DeepSeek-Coder-V2-Lite is a 16B MoE with ~2.4B active, so it decodes about as fast as a 2-3B dense model on my setup (~70 tok/s) while coding like something larger.
label: ANALYSIS

text: I measured TTFT vs total latency: on a 2k-token prompt, prefill was 0.4s and generation of 300 tokens was 4s. So for short outputs prompt length dominates, for long outputs generation does.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct beat Llama-3.1-70B on my 50-item function-calling eval (46 vs 39 valid calls) — it follows the JSON schema more reliably, fewer hallucinated arg names.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Setting rope_freq_base manually let me push Mistral past its trained context with less degradation than naive linear scaling — needle recall held to ~24k vs collapsing at 16k before.
label: ANALYSIS

text: A 70B at Q4_K_M is ~40GB, so it does NOT fit one 24GB card — you need two, or heavy CPU offload that drops you to single-digit tok/s. People claiming '70B on a 3090' are running tiny i-quants.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Whisper's word-error-rate jumped on my noisy field recordings (12% -> 31%) until I added a denoise pass; the model is trained mostly on clean audio, so SNR matters more than model size here.
label: ANALYSIS

text: I A/B tested greedy vs min-p 0.05 sampling on story generation: min-p had far fewer degenerate repetitions while keeping coherence, because it prunes the long low-probability tail dynamically.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Mixtral's router sends each token to 2 of 8 experts, so per-token FLOPs ≈ a 13B dense model even though VRAM has to hold all 47B. That's the whole speed/memory tradeoff in one sentence.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Converting my LoRA to GGUF and merging it let me run the finetune in llama.cpp with no adapter-loading overhead; inference matched the base model's speed exactly (tested, 0% slowdown).
label: ANALYSIS

text: On an RTX 3060 12GB, 13B Q4_K_M with 24 layers offloaded gave me 18 tok/s; full GPU offload OOM'd. The partial-offload bottleneck is the per-token CPU<->GPU copy for the CPU layers.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Phi-3.5-mini scored higher than Llama-3-8B on GSM8K in my run (78% vs 71%) but lower on my open-ended support tickets — synthetic-heavy training helps clean benchmarks more than messy real tasks.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Prompt caching cut my agent's cost ~70% because the 3k-token tool+system prefix is identical every turn, so only the new user message gets billed at full prefill rate.
label: ANALYSIS

text: I checked the actual paper: the '90% HumanEval' figure is pass@10 with sampling, not pass@1. Single-shot pass@1 in the same table is 61%, which is a very different claim.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Stop sequences matter for throughput in agents — without a tight stop token my model kept generating past the answer, wasting ~80 tokens per call. Adding the right stop saved real latency.
label: ANALYSIS

text: KV-cache quantization to Q8 let me fit 32k context in 24GB where fp16 cache OOM'd at ~20k; needle recall was unchanged in my test, so Q8 cache is basically free here.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Llama-3-8B and Mistral-7B have nearly identical params but Llama's bigger vocab (128k vs 32k) means it processes my English corpus in ~12% fewer tokens — measured on the same 10k docs.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Continuous batching is why my server's p99 latency dropped under load: finished sequences free their slot immediately instead of the whole batch waiting for the longest one. Saw p99 fall ~40%.
label: ANALYSIS

text: I ran the same prompt at temp 0 five times and got identical output (as expected with greedy), but at temp 0.7 got 5 different answers — so 'flaky' results in a pipeline are almost always nonzero temp.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Command-R was clearly better at my grounded-RAG eval (cited the right chunk 44/50) than Llama-3-8B (31/50) — it's specifically trained with citation/grounding objectives, which shows.
label: ANALYSIS

text: My finetune's accuracy on the held-out set was 91% but only 73% on real production traffic — distribution shift. The training data was cleaner and shorter than what users actually send.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Loading a model with device_map='auto' on transformers split it across my 2 GPUs but ran them sequentially (one idle at a time); only vLLM's tensor parallelism actually used both at once. Watched nvidia-smi.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Speculative decoding with a Q4 draft and Q8 target still gave ~1.6x on my code workload because acceptance was high (~75%); the draft's lower precision barely changed which tokens it proposes.
label: ANALYSIS

text: IQ3_XXS on a 70B gave me 13/20 on my reasoning rubric in 22GB, beating an 8B Q8 (11/20) in the same VRAM — i-quants with imatrix preserve more than naive Q3 at that bitrate.
label: ANALYSIS

text: The model looped on long outputs until I added presence_penalty 0.3; greedy decoding plus quantization flattens close logits, so ties resolve to repeats without a penalty.
label: ANALYSIS

text: nf4 (bitsandbytes) dequantizes to bf16 every forward pass, so it's slower than a GGUF Q4 whose kernels compute on the quantized blocks directly — I saw ~25% lower tok/s with nf4 at equal quality.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Yi-1.5-9B held facts at 32k context in my needle test (88% recall at the far end) where rope-scaled Mistral collapsed to ~50% — Yi was actually pretrained long, not extrapolated.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Batch size 8 vs 16 in my LoRA run changed nothing on final accuracy but halved memory headroom; the real lever was learning rate — 1e-4 diverged, 2e-5 was stable. Logged both runs.
label: ANALYSIS

text: vLLM prefix caching took my chatbot's TTFT from 1.1s to 0.18s on repeat turns because the long shared system prompt's KV gets reused instead of recomputed each call.
label: ANALYSIS

text: The P40 is 24GB but its fp16 throughput is 1/64 of fp32, so modern fp16 kernels crawl on it — that's why people report it being slow despite the VRAM. It's an int8/fp32 card.
label: ANALYSIS

text: I distilled my 70B's labels into a DistilBERT classifier and it hit 94% of the 70B's accuracy at 1/100th the inference cost — for a fixed-label task the big model is overkill at serve time.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Gemma-2 needs logit soft-capping at inference; an old llama.cpp build without it gave me gibberish, updating fixed it. The capping is part of the architecture, not optional.
label: ANALYSIS

text: On my 4090, FP8 inference (native on Ada) ran ~1.4x faster than fp16 with no measurable quality loss on my eval; on a 3090 FP8 was slower because Ampere emulates it. Same code, different silicon.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Reducing max_new_tokens doesn't raise tok/s — it just stops earlier. My per-token rate stayed 42 tok/s whether the cap was 128 or 512; total time scaled with tokens actually generated.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Qwen2.5-Coder-7B fixed 16/20 of my real bug-repro tasks vs DeepSeek-Coder-6.7B's 12/20, but DeepSeek won on fill-in-the-middle — different FIM training objectives, visible in the failures.
label: ANALYSIS

text: My RAG answers got worse when I raised top-k from 4 to 12: more chunks meant more distractors and the model averaged over them. Recall@k went up but answer accuracy went down. Measured both.
label: ANALYSIS

text: LoRA rank 8 vs 64 on my 1k-example set: rank 8 generalized better (val acc 89 vs 84), rank 64 memorized. For small data, lower rank is a regularizer, not a limitation.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Tokenizing Chinese, Llama-3 used ~40% fewer tokens than Llama-2 on the same passage, so the effective context and cost improved a lot for CJK even with the same window number.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Running --n-gpu-layers 99 on an 8GB card silently spilled to shared system memory on Windows and tanked speed to 1 tok/s; capping at the layers that actually fit (28) gave 14 tok/s.
label: ANALYSIS

text: DRY sampling beat repetition_penalty for my long-form generation because it penalizes repeated n-grams, not individual tokens, so it stopped phrase-level loops without nuking normal word reuse.
label: ANALYSIS

text: I benchmarked exllamav2 vs llama.cpp on a pure-GPU 13B: exl2 78 tok/s, llama.cpp 64. But the moment I needed CPU offload, exl2 wasn't an option, so llama.cpp won by default there.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Setting num_ctx=8192 in Ollama instead of the 2048 default stopped my long prompts from being silently truncated — that's why earlier answers 'forgot' the start of my input.
label: ANALYSIS

text: The instruct model refused a benign medical-summary task until I removed the default safety preamble; on 30 such prompts refusals dropped from 11 to 2. The preamble itself was the trigger.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Grouped-query attention gives Llama-3-70B 8 KV heads instead of 64, so its KV cache is ~8x smaller than full multi-head would be at the same context — that's why it fits where you'd expect it not to.
label: ANALYSIS

text: My agent was slow because each step re-embedded the entire conversation for memory search; caching embeddings of past turns cut per-step latency from 900ms to 120ms. Profiled it.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Q5_K_M vs Q6_K on a 34B: I couldn't separate them on quality (both 17/20) so Q5 wins on size and speed. Above Q5 the returns are basically noise on my eval.
label: ANALYSIS

text: vLLM hit ~2x the tokens/sec of TGI on the same 4090 and model in my load test, mostly due to more aggressive continuous batching and paged attention. Same prompts, same hardware.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Whisper large-v3 vs distil-large-v2 on clean English: WER 4.1% vs 4.6%, but distil ran 4x faster on my 3060. For clear speech the distilled decoder layers barely matter.
label: ANALYSIS

text: I measured that my 'long context' degradation was actually lost-in-the-middle: recall was 92% for facts at the start/end of a 32k doc but 48% in the middle. Reordering key facts to the edges fixed it.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Mistral-Nemo's 12B fit my 12GB card at Q4 with 16k context and beat Llama-3-8B on my multilingual eval by ~15 points — the larger multilingual pretraining mix carries non-English.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Using an imatrix built from my own domain text, my Q4 quant scored 2/20 higher than a generic Q4 on in-domain prompts; the importance matrix spent precision where my data needed it.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Flash attention isn't available on Pascal/P40 (no supported kernels), which is the actual reason your modern 3090 crushes it even at equal VRAM — not just raw clock speed.
label: ANALYSIS

text: I logged GPU util during inference: single-stream llama.cpp sat at ~30% util (memory-bandwidth bound on decode), while batched vLLM pushed it to ~95%. That gap is the throughput difference.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Switching from cosine to constant LR with warmup made my short finetune more stable — cosine decayed too fast on 300 steps and underfit. Final val acc went 82 -> 88.
label: ANALYSIS

text: DeepSeek-V2.5 answered my repo-level questions correctly only when I fit the relevant files in context; truncating to 8k dropped accuracy from 17/20 to 9/20. The capability needs the context.
label: ANALYSIS

text: int4 GPTQ vs int4 AWQ on my 4090: AWQ decoded ~10% faster at equal accuracy because it keeps salient channels in fp16 and uses a simpler kernel. Benchmarked 200 prompts each.
label: ANALYSIS

text: My TTS latency was dominated by the vocoder, not the acoustic model — swapping to a faster vocoder cut end-to-end from 1.3s to 0.5s per utterance with no audible quality drop.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Llama-3-8B-Instruct's chat template uses specific header tokens; feeding raw text without them dropped my instruction-following accuracy hard (it completed instead of answered). Applying the template fixed it.
label: ANALYSIS

text: I checked: the model's 'state of the art on MMLU' claim used a different few-shot count than the comparison model. Normalizing to 5-shot for both, the gap shrank from 6 points to under 1.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Quantizing only the weights but keeping the KV cache fp16 is why your '4-bit' 70B still uses 25GB at long context — the cache isn't quantized, and it grows with sequence length.
label: ANALYSIS

text: On my 2x3090 NVLink rig, tensor parallelism gave ~1.9x on 70B in vLLM, but llama.cpp's layer split only ~1.3x because layers run sequentially across cards rather than in parallel. Same hardware.
label: ANALYSIS

text: A 1.1 repetition penalty fixed my code model emitting endless comment blocks; without it greedy decoding kept picking the slightly-highest repeated token. Tiny change, big behavior shift.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Base Llama-3 'can't chat' because it was only trained to complete text; few-shot Q/A formatting made it answer fine, which proves it's a formatting gap, not a capability one. Tested directly.
label: ANALYSIS

text: My embedding retrieval improved a lot after I normalized vectors and switched to cosine — with raw dot-product, longer docs dominated by magnitude. Recall@5 went 0.71 -> 0.86.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Gemma-2-27B beat the 9B on my hardest reasoning subset (24/30 vs 17/30) but they tied on easy factual recall — size buys robustness on the ambiguous items, not the lookups.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Running two models on one GPU with vLLM's --gpu-memory-utilization 0.45 each worked but each got OOM-prone at long context; the cap leaves no room for KV growth. Had to lower max context.
label: ANALYSIS

text: I timed cold start: loading a 13B Q4 from NVMe took 4s, from a spinning disk 28s. If your 'model is slow' complaint is about the first request, it's disk I/O, not compute.
label: ANALYSIS

text: FP16 vs bf16 on my finetune: bf16 was more stable (no loss spikes) because of its wider exponent range, even though it has fewer mantissa bits. Same final accuracy, fewer NaNs.
label: ANALYSIS

text: The '128k context' worked in my long-doc QA up to ~100k (85% recall) on Llama-3.1 but throughput fell to 3 tok/s there, so it's usable but slow — both things are true, I measured them.
label: ANALYSIS

text: Mixtral drifted from my requested output format more often than Llama-3-70B (formatting errors 14% vs 5% over 80 prompts) even though it matched on knowledge — instruction adherence differs from facts.
label: ANALYSIS
